seo.ua.title: User-Agent Parser & Browser Diagnostics | ToolBoxNova
seo.ua.description: Parse any User-Agent string and inspect your live browser environment. Detects browser, OS, device, engine, bot status, and User-Agent Client Hints. 100% client-side.

seo.ua.guide.title: User-Agent String Guide for Developers | ToolBoxNova
seo.ua.guide.description: A practical reference on User-Agent strings: anatomy, parsing rules, bot detection, User-Agent Client Hints, spoofing limits, and developer use cases.

ua.accuracy.title: Accuracy & limitations
ua.accuracy.body1: User-Agent strings are self-reported and trivially spoofable. Treat them as a hint, never as authoritative identity.
ua.accuracy.body2: For high-stakes checks combine the UA with feature detection, behavioral signals, and User-Agent Client Hints when available.
ua.accuracy.body3: Niche browsers, Embedded WebViews, and headless tools may pass through generic UA values that look benign — keep that in mind when triaging traffic.

ua.step1.title: Paste or auto-detect
ua.step1.desc: Paste any UA string into the input field, or click Detect My UA to capture the current tab's User-Agent.
ua.step2.title: Instant parsing
ua.step2.desc: The engine runs entirely in your browser using regex rules to extract browser, OS, device, engine, and architecture.

ua.privacy.label: Privacy first
ua.privacy.title: Your data never leaves your browser
ua.privacy.body1: All parsing, environment readings, and Client Hints lookups happen locally in JavaScript. No string is ever transmitted to our servers.
ua.privacy.body2: That makes the tool safe for analyzing internal UAs, mobile app identifiers, or other strings you'd rather not paste into a remote API.

ua.guide.hero.title: The developer's guide to User-Agent strings
ua.guide.hero.subtitle: How browsers identify themselves, why parsing is tricky, what User-Agent Client Hints change, and where UA-based detection should never stand alone.
ua.guide.toc.title: On this page
ua.guide.s1.title: What a User-Agent really is
ua.guide.s1.p1: A User-Agent (UA) is a free-form HTTP header that every browser, mobile app, command-line client, and crawler attaches to outbound requests. Its job is to identify the software making the request so the server can decide which content, layout, or capabilities to deliver.
ua.guide.s1.p2: The header dates back to early Mozilla — that is why nearly every modern browser still leads with the legacy token Mozilla/5.0. The rest of the string accumulated over decades of compatibility hacks, which is precisely why parsing it correctly is harder than it looks.
ua.guide.s2.title: Anatomy of a UA string
ua.guide.s2.p1: A typical Chrome on Windows UA looks like this:
ua.guide.s2.code: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36
ua.guide.s2.li1: Mozilla/5.0 — a historical compatibility prefix that almost every browser still sends.
ua.guide.s2.li2: (...) — the platform comment block: OS family, version, CPU architecture.
ua.guide.s2.li3: AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) — engine identification, also a compatibility legacy.
ua.guide.s2.li4: Chrome/124.0.0.0 — the browser name and version.
ua.guide.s2.li5: Safari/537.36 — another compatibility token, retained because old sniffers expect it.
ua.guide.s3.title: Parsing rules that matter
ua.guide.s3.p1: Order is everything. Parse Edge before Chrome, Opera (OPR) before Chrome, and Chromium before Chrome — otherwise everything looks like Chrome. Mobile Safari is matched on Version/X.Y plus Safari, not Safari alone, because every WebView ships Safari in its UA.
ua.guide.s3.p2: Operating systems hide in the parenthesized block. Windows uses NT kernel numbers — NT 10.0 covers both Windows 10 and 11 (Windows 11 is not currently signaled in the UA). iOS uses underscores, not dots: 17_4 means 17.4. macOS prefixes the version with Mac OS X. Android exposes its real version (Android 14, etc.).
ua.guide.s3.p3: Device classification is inferred. iPhone, iPad, iPod, plus Mobile-tagged Android, are mobile. Macintosh, Windows, X11, and CrOS land on desktop. Tablets are tricky — iPads on iPadOS 13+ even pretend to be Macs by default.
ua.guide.s3.p4: Engine matching is anchored to identifiable tokens: Blink for Chromium, WebKit for Safari, Gecko for Firefox, Trident/EdgeHTML for legacy IE/Edge. Always extract version from the engine token, not from the browser version.
ua.guide.s4.title: Bot and crawler detection
ua.guide.s4.p1: Most well-behaved bots announce themselves: Googlebot, bingbot, DuckDuckBot, YandexBot, Baiduspider, AhrefsBot, SemrushBot, GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, and so on. A simple substring match on the lowercase UA covers the majority.
ua.guide.s4.p2: Headless tooling leaks too — HeadlessChrome, PhantomJS, Selenium, and Puppeteer often appear inside an otherwise-legitimate Chrome UA. Combine that signal with TLS fingerprinting and request-rate analysis for stronger guarantees.
ua.guide.s5.title: User-Agent Client Hints
ua.guide.s5.p1: Client Hints are the modern replacement for UA sniffing. Chromium browsers expose navigator.userAgentData with structured fields — brands, mobile flag, platform — and a getHighEntropyValues() method for richer details such as platform version, architecture, model, and bitness.
ua.guide.s5.p2: Server-side, Chromium sends Sec-CH-UA, Sec-CH-UA-Mobile, and Sec-CH-UA-Platform automatically. Higher-entropy fields like Sec-CH-UA-Model are sent only when the server requests them via the Accept-CH response header. Firefox and Safari do not currently implement Client Hints.
ua.guide.s6.title: Spoofing and limitations
ua.guide.s6.p1: Any HTTP client can set an arbitrary UA. Bots impersonate browsers, browsers offer privacy modes that freeze the UA, and tools like curl or Python's requests start with no UA at all. Treat UA-based decisions as best-effort hints.
ua.guide.s6.p2: For security-sensitive checks, augment UA detection with behavioral signals, IP reputation, fingerprinting, and Client Hints validation — and never gate authentication on a UA value alone.
ua.guide.s7.title: Common developer use cases
ua.guide.s7.p1: Serving responsive layouts to mobile browsers without waiting for layout viewport queries. Filtering bot traffic from analytics. Capability-flagging legacy browsers in legacy admin dashboards. Generating Accept-CH headers to opt into the Client Hints fields you actually consume.
ua.guide.s7.p2: On the debugging side: reproducing bug reports by switching UA in DevTools, validating that headless test runners get the right experience, and capturing UA-rich support snapshots from end users.
ua.guide.cta.title: Ready to inspect a UA?
ua.guide.cta.subtitle: Open the parser, paste any User-Agent, and read the structured result.
